title: Managing mediocrity: Indonesia’s defence-partnership portfolio
gist: Indonesia has signed 87 bilateral defence and security commitments with 36 countries in 25 years, plus three major 2026 deals with Australia, the US and Japan, but IISS argues this 'paper portfolio' of mostly non-binding MOUs, spread across too many diverse partners, fails to enhance military effectiveness and leaves Jakarta with interoperability problems and a mediocre, siloed defence posture.

themes.theme: The 2026 defence deals and hedging posture

insights.claim: In early 2026 Indonesia signed the Treaty on Common Security with Australia (February), the Major Defence Cooperation Partnership with the United States (April), and a Defence Cooperation Arrangement with Japan (May), the latter coinciding with Japan relaxing its arms-export restrictions.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: A cluster of major Indonesian defence deals signals deepening Indo-Pacific alignment and opens defence-industrial and arms-export opportunities, especially for Japan post-liberalization.
insights.quote: In April, Indonesia signed the Major Defence Cooperation Partnership with the United States, anchored around military modernisation, exercises, education and training.
insights.locator: Jakarta has recently signed a string of major defence deals.

insights.claim: The US-Indonesia partnership commits both countries to explore co-developing asymmetric capabilities in maritime, subsurface and autonomous-systems domains, plus maintenance, repair and overhaul support.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Co-development in maritime, subsurface and autonomous systems flags areas of potential US-Indonesia defence-industrial collaboration to watch for contractors.
insights.quote: both countries will also explore 'co-developing sophisticated asymmetric capabilities … in the maritime, subsurface, and autonomous systems domains'
insights.locator: Jakarta has recently signed a string of major defence deals.

insights.claim: The three new deals do not fundamentally change Indonesia's mediocre defence-partnership portfolio or substantially enhance its military effectiveness, because Jakarta lacks a fully developed strategy amid a disordered strategic-policymaking ecosystem.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Headline deals overstate real alignment; Indonesia remains a hedging, non-committal partner, tempering expectations of a decisive Indo-Pacific tilt.
insights.quote: the three new deals do not fundamentally change the country's diverse but arguably mediocre defence-partnership portfolio
insights.locator: All of this assumes that Jakarta has a fully developed

insights.claim: Indonesia publicly signed 87 bilateral defence and security cooperation commitments with 36 countries between 2001 and 2025, but most were non-binding MOUs, joint statements, letters of intent or administrative arrangements with few if any legally binding commitments.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: The bulk of Indonesia's defence agreements are non-binding, so their existence is a weak signal of genuine strategic alignment or guaranteed procurement.
insights.quote: Indonesia has publicly signed 87 bilateral defence and security cooperation commitments with 36 countries between 2001 and 2025.

insights.claim: Indonesia's non-alignment instinct means its commitments will not automatically translate into advanced platforms, broad operational access, deep industrial collaboration, joint contingency planning or top-level intelligence sharing.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Non-alignment caps the depth of any partnership; expect limited intelligence-sharing and contingency planning even with the US and Australia.
insights.quote: the commitments will not directly or automatically translate into advanced defence platforms, broad operational access, deep industrial collaboration, joint contingency planning or top-level intelligence sharing
insights.locator: Indonesia's deeply entrenched instinct towards non-alignment

insights.claim: Indonesia has signed more defence and security commitments with Australia (at least 10) than any other country, with China close second at 8 documents, Russia at 7, the US at 6 and Japan at 5.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: China ranking second only to Australia in agreement count underscores Indonesia's genuine multi-vector hedging, not a clean Western tilt.
insights.quote: Indonesia has signed more defence and security commitments with Australia (at least 10) than any other country; China comes a close second with 8 documents
insights.locator: Furthermore, the states that Indonesia signed deals with

insights.claim: Indonesia uses a 'splitting the spoils' retail approach where different partners supply different defence components, with the US and Australia as top providers of exercises, education and training and France, Italy, Russia, South Korea, Türkiye, the UK and US as top weapons suppliers.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Fragmented sourcing means no single supplier dominates Indonesian procurement; opportunity is diffuse but so is leverage for any one defence exporter.
insights.quote: Different partners provide different goods, which aligns with Indonesia's 'retail approach' to defence planning
insights.locator: In short, there is no single top provider

insights.claim: Indonesia's haphazard, overly diversified weapons systems from dozens of countries do not work well together by officials' private admission, creating path-dependent interoperability challenges at both platform and organizational levels.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Interoperability dysfunction limits Indonesia's actual combat readiness regardless of headline procurement, a structural weakness in its defence value.
insights.quote: The Indonesian military operates an array of haphazard, overly diversified weapons systems from dozens of countries that officials privately admit do not really work well together.
insights.locator: The plethora of written defence and security commitments

insights.claim: More than 7,000 Indonesian students studied at over 200 US professional military education programmes from 2002 to 2018, yet officers returning from overseas training continue to be deployed for domestic socio-political-economic tasks.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Deep US training ties have not translated into externally focused capability, signaling that investment in Indonesian human capital yields limited strategic returns.
insights.quote: More than 7,000 Indonesian students studied at over 200 US professional military education programmes from 2002 to 2018.
insights.locator: The US and Australia, for example, have been the top providers

insights.claim: Without a mature strategic-thought process, Indonesia reacts to rather than proactively shapes defence-partnership offers, leaving it with mediocre partnerships that meet siloed needs but do not fundamentally enhance overall defence capability and readiness.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Indonesia's reactive posture means partners can shape outcomes if proactive; the burden of strategy falls on counterparties seeking deeper ties.
insights.quote: Indonesia often reacts to – rather than proactively shapes – defence-partnership offers.
